questions.stem: A 22-year-old male presents to the emergency department after a fall onto his outstretched right hand while playing football. He has tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox and pain on axial loading of the thumb. Initial plain radiographs of the wrist are normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox and pain on axial loading of the thumb are classic for scaphoid fracture, even with normal X-rays (occult fracture). Scapholunate ligament injury typically presents with a positive Watson scaphoid shift test rather than snuffbox tenderness. De Quervain's tenosynovitis causes pain on the radial side of the wrist with Finkelstein's test, and thumb CMC dislocation would cause deformity and loss of joint space.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman presents after a fall onto her left hand. She has swelling and tenderness over the dorsal aspect of the wrist, but no snuffbox tenderness. She has paraesthesia in the median nerve distribution. X-ray reveals a loss of normal carpal arc and a triangular appearance of the lunate on the AP view. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The triangular appearance of the lunate on AP view (piece-of-pie sign) and loss of carpal arcs are classic for lunate dislocation. Median nerve symptoms are common. Scaphoid fracture would have snuffbox tenderness. Perilunate dislocation typically has a spilt-lunate sign and scaphoid fracture is often associated. Triquetral fracture presents with dorsal tenderness.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old mechanic presents with persistent radial-sided wrist pain following a fall 3 months ago. He was initially treated in a cast for a 'scaphoid fracture' but has not improved. Examination reveals tenderness and a palpable prominence over the snuffbox. X-ray shows a sclerotic proximal pole and a lucent line across the scaphoid waist with cystic changes. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The history of a prior scaphoid fracture with non-healing, persistent snuffbox tenderness, and X-ray findings of sclerotic proximal pole (AVN) combined with a lucent fracture line and cysts indicate scaphoid non-union. AVN alone would show increased density without a visible fracture line. Kienböck's disease involves the lunate. SLAC is degenerative from scapholunate dissociation.

questions.stem: A 12-year-old boy presents after falling off his bicycle onto his right hand. He complains of wrist pain and has mild swelling over the dorsoradial aspect. There is tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox. Plain radiographs are normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In children, scaphoid fractures can be missed on plain X-rays due to incomplete ossification. Tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox is highly suggestive. Distal radial buckle fracture typically presents with tenderness over the distal radius, not specifically snuffbox. Triquetral avulsion fractures cause dorsal tenderness. Wrist sprain would not have such specific tenderness.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with acute wrist pain after a fall on an outstretched hand. She has tenderness over the scaphoid tubercle and pain on ulnar deviation. X-ray shows a clear fracture through the waist of the scaphoid with no displacement. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: A clear fracture line through the waist on X-ray with no displacement confirms a non-displaced scaphoid waist fracture. Proximal pole fractures are more likely to have displacement or AVN. Tubercle fractures are extra-articular. Bennett's and Rolando fractures involve the thumb metacarpal base.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department after falling onto his outstretched right hand. He has tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox and pain on axial loading of the thumb. Plain radiographs (posteroanterior, lateral, and scaphoid views) show no fracture. What is the single most appropriate next investigation?
questions.explanation: MRI has the highest sensitivity and specificity for occult scaphoid fractures and is the recommended next step when initial X-rays are normal but clinical suspicion remains high. Repeat X-ray delays diagnosis; CT is less sensitive for occult fractures.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents with persistent wrist pain 4 months after a scaphoid waist fracture treated conservatively. X-ray shows a gap at the fracture site with cystic changes. What is the best investigation to confirm non-union and guide further management?
questions.explanation: CT provides the best bony detail to assess the fracture gap, sclerosis, and cystic changes, making it the gold standard for evaluating scaphoid non-union. MRI is better for assessing avascular necrosis but bony architecture is best seen on CT.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man attends the fracture clinic after a fall. The scaphoid X-ray shows a thin, linear radiolucency across the waist. Which of the following features would confirm this is a fracture rather than a nutrient vessel or vascular groove?
options: The line is parallel to the radial border of the scaphoid
The line is irregular and non-corticated
The line is located in the proximal pole
The line extends from the dorsal to the volar cortex
The line is smooth and corticated
questions.explanation: True fractures are irregular and have sharp, non-corticated edges, whereas nutrient vessels appear as smooth, corticated lines. A line that is irregular and non-corticated is diagnostic of fracture.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with snuffbox tenderness after a fall. X-ray shows a scaphoid waist fracture. To assess displacement or angulation of the fracture fragments, which investigation should be performed?
questions.explanation: CT provides multiplanar reformats allowing accurate measurement of displacement and angulation, essential for surgical planning. MRI is not as good for detailed bony alignment.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man returns to the fracture clinic 6 months after a scaphoid waist fracture that was managed in a cast. He reports ongoing wrist pain. X-ray shows a lucent line at the fracture site with surrounding sclerosis and small cystic lucencies. Which of the following best describes this appearance?
options: Avascular necrosis of the proximal pole
questions.explanation: The combination of a persistent lucent fracture line, sclerosis, and cystic changes is classic for established non-union. Malunion implies healing in a deformed position; avascular necrosis typically shows increased density of the proximal pole without a lucent line.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man presents with snuffbox tenderness after a fall. X-ray confirms a non-displaced scaphoid waist fracture. What is the first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Non-displaced scaphoid waist fractures have good union rates with cast immobilisation; a below-elbow thumb spica cast is standard. Above-elbow cast offers no advantage. Surgery is reserved for displaced fractures or delayed union.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old builder sustains a scaphoid waist fracture with 2 mm displacement and comminution. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
questions.explanation: Displaced scaphoid fractures (gap >1 mm) have high non-union rates with casting; operative fixation (percutaneous screw) is recommended. Casting is reserved for non-displaced fractures. Excision/arthrodesis are salvage procedures.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman had a scaphoid fracture treated in a cast 6 months ago. She now has pain and X-ray shows non-union with avascular necrosis of the proximal pole. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Non-vascularised bone grafting with screw fixation
Vascularised bone graft from the distal radius
questions.explanation: For scaphoid non-union without significant deformity, conventional bone grafting (Matti-Russe) with internal fixation is first-line. Vascularised graft is reserved for failed prior grafting or extensive avascular necrosis. Salvage procedures (proximal row carpectomy) are for advanced collapse.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with known osteoporosis is diagnosed with a non-displaced scaphoid waist fracture. He has mild wrist pain. Which of the following is contraindicated in his management?
options: Oral calcium and vitamin D supplementation
questions.explanation: NSAIDs can impair bone healing and are relatively contraindicated in fracture management, especially in patients with risk factors for non-union. Cast immobilisation and calcium/vitamin D are appropriate. Early exercises are not recommended for a fresh fracture.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old professional rugby player has a non-displaced scaphoid waist fracture. He wants to return to play as quickly as possible. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Below-elbow thumb spica cast for 8 weeks
Excision of the scaphoid with silicone replacement
questions.explanation: In high-demand athletes, operative fixation of non-displaced scaphoid fractures allows earlier return to sport (4-6 weeks) compared to casting (8-12 weeks). Casting is standard for non-athletes, but surgery is preferred for faster recovery. Wrist splint and excision are not appropriate.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old student presents to ED after falling onto her outstretched right hand. She has snuffbox tenderness and pain on axial compression of the thumb. Initial scaphoid view X-ray shows no fracture. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Request a CT scan of the wrist
Apply a below-elbow thumb spica cast
questions.explanation: In suspected scaphoid fracture with normal initial X-ray, the false-negative rate is high (up to 15%). Current UK guidance recommends immobilisation in a scaphoid cast and repeat X-ray in 10-14 days. Immediate MRI is not routinely available and would delay definitive management, while reassurance risks displacement.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old builder presents with a non-displaced scaphoid waist fracture confirmed on X-ray. There is no carpal instability or dislocation. Examination shows mild swelling and tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Request an MRI for ligamentous injury
Perform open reduction and internal fixation
Request a CT scan to assess displacement
Advise wrist splint and early mobilisation
Apply a below-elbow thumb spica cast
questions.explanation: Non-displaced scaphoid waist fractures are managed conservatively with a below-elbow thumb spica cast for 6-8 weeks. Surgery is reserved for displaced fractures or non-union. CT or MRI are unnecessary if standard views are adequate.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old motorcyclist is brought to ED after a high-energy crash. He has a swollen, deformed right wrist with paraesthesia in the median nerve distribution. X-ray shows a scaphoid fracture associated with a perilunate dislocation. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform open reduction and internal fixation
Perform immediate closed reduction of the dislocation
Apply a splint and arrange urgent follow-up
Request an urgent CT to assess fracture geometry
Request an MRI to evaluate scapholunate ligament
questions.explanation: Perilunate dislocation is an orthopaedic emergency requiring prompt closed reduction to relieve pressure on the median nerve and prevent avascular necrosis. Imaging delays would worsen nerve injury; open reduction is not first-line in the acute setting.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old manual worker had a scaphoid waist fracture treated in a cast for 12 weeks. He now has persistent radial wrist pain and tenderness. Repeat X-ray shows a clear non-union at the fracture site with no signs of avascular necrosis. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Request an MRI to assess vascularity
Arrange for pulsed electromagnetic field therapy
Request a CT scan for further characterisation
Refer for surgical fixation and possible bone grafting
questions.explanation: Established non-union after adequate immobilisation (12 weeks) rarely heals with continued casting. Surgery (e.g., Herbert screw fixation ± bone graft) is the standard of care. CT or MRI may be considered preoperatively but the immediate next step is referral for surgery.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old retired gardener falls from standing height onto her right hand. X-ray shows a minimally displaced scaphoid tubercle fracture. She is independent but has low functional demand and osteoporosis. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Advise a wrist splint and early mobilisation
Apply a below-elbow thumb spica cast
Request a CT to exclude displacement
No treatment; reassure and advise analgesia
questions.explanation: Even in elderly patients with low demand, scaphoid fractures are usually immobilised in a cast to prevent non-union. The tubercle fracture is stable, but conservative management is still first-line. Surgery is reserved for displaced fractures or failed conservative treatment, and no treatment risks non-union.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man presents to the ED after falling onto his outstretched left hand. He has mild wrist pain but no swelling or deformity. Which clinical finding is most suggestive of a scaphoid fracture?
questions.explanation: Anatomical snuffbox tenderness is the most sensitive and specific clinical sign for scaphoid fracture. Radial styloid tenderness is more typical of radial styloid fracture; thumb extension pain suggests De Quervain's tenosynovitis; ulnar styloid tenderness is for ulnar-sided injuries; wrist flexion pain is non-specific.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman is being treated for a non-displaced scaphoid waist fracture with a below-elbow cast. She returns for a 2-week follow-up. Which finding suggests a higher risk of nonunion and warrants further imaging or prolonged immobilisation?
options: Decreased range of motion at the wrist
Persistent tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox
questions.explanation: Persistent anatomical snuffbox tenderness at 2 weeks is a red flag for delayed union or nonunion, indicating the need for further imaging or extended immobilisation. Callus formation is a good sign of healing; mild swelling and decreased ROM are expected; thumb flexion pain is not specific.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with a scaphoid fracture presents 5 days after injury with increasing pain, marked swelling, and a tense wrist. Which clinical sign is most concerning for acute compartment syndrome?
options: Pain on passive extension of the fingers
Paraesthesia in the median nerve distribution
questions.explanation: Pain on passive stretch of the fingers is the earliest and most sensitive sign of compartment syndrome. Paraesthesia is a later sign; absent pulse, delayed capillary refill, and pallor are very late signs representing irreversible ischaemia.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with persistent wrist pain 6 months after a scaphoid fracture that was treated conservatively. X-ray shows a scaphoid waist fracture with a gap and sclerotic changes in the proximal pole. Which finding indicates avascular necrosis of the proximal pole?
options: Cystic changes in the distal pole
Sclerosis (increased density) of the proximal pole
questions.explanation: Sclerosis of the proximal pole is the hallmark radiographic sign of avascular necrosis due to relative increased density of dead bone. Radiolucency and cysts are features of nonunion but not specific for AVN; displacement is a risk factor for nonunion; bony bridging indicates healing.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with osteoporosis falls and lands on her outstretched hand. X-ray reveals a displaced scaphoid waist fracture with 2 mm of offset. Which radiographic finding is a threshold for considering surgical fixation due to high risk of nonunion?
questions.explanation: Displacement >1 mm is a widely accepted threshold for surgical fixation as it significantly increases nonunion risk. Proximal pole fractures also have high risk but are not a threshold; gap >1 mm, comminution, and humpback deformity are risk factors but not the classic primary threshold.
